description: Figure 2 presents the room-temperature tensile mechanical response of an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C (wt.%) low-density steel comparing uncharged and hydrogen-charged (101 mass ppm) conditions tested at an initial strain rate of 5.0×10⁻⁵ s⁻¹ at 295 K. Panel (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves where the hydrogen-charged sample (blue curve) exhibits slightly elevated yield strength (~302 MPa versus ~275 MPa) and ultimate tensile strength (~595 MPa versus ~580 MPa) compared to the uncharged sample (red curve), concomitant with improved ductility evidenced by increased uniform elongation from 0.43 to 0.46. Panel (b) displays true stress-strain curves (solid lines) and the corresponding strain-hardening rate curves (dotted lines, labeled SH-Uncharged and SH-Hydrogen-charged), revealing four distinct strain-hardening stages (A, B, C, D) in both conditions. The strain-hardening behavior remains qualitatively similar upon hydrogen charging, showing no additional hardening stage associated with deformation twinning, consistent with the material's high stacking fault energy. The curves demonstrate that hydrogen marginally enhances the strain-hardening rate at early deformation stages while preserving the characteristic four-stage hardening sequence, supporting the conclusion that hydrogen promotes microbanding mechanisms without introducing embrittlement.
